title: Collective deformation of anisotropic particles with internal pulsation
authors: Luca Casagrande, Alessandro Manacorda, Etienne Fodor
abstract: Capturing the emergence of deformation waves in contractile living tissues is a challenge that has recently been tackled with models of actively deformable particles. Inspired by the anisotropic deformation of cardiomyocytes in cardiac tissues, we examine how the pulsation of elliptical particles affects their collective properties in dense assemblies. We introduce two types of deformation where the eccentricity of each particle is subject to a periodic drive, and examine the interplay between nematic order and synchronized deformation via a systematic phase diagram. We derive a hydrodynamic description through a coarse-graining procedure, and show that it qualitatively captures the main collective states of the microscopic dynamics. Overall, our model provides key insights into how an active anisotropic deformation yields waves that self-organize into various dynamical patterns.
summary: This paper investigates how the shape-changing pulsations of anisotropic particles lead to complex collective behaviors like deformation waves and nematic order. By comparing squeezing and stretching modes, it demonstrates how internal particle dynamics can drive large-scale pattern formation in active matter.
main_problem: Understanding how the anisotropic deformation of pulsating particles affects their collective properties and the emergence of deformation waves in dense assemblies.
main_result: The study identifies various collective states including arrested, cycling, and deformation wave phases, showing that squeezing-type deformation uniquely allows for a nematic phase at high densities.
method: The authors use numerical simulations of overdamped Langevin dynamics and derive a hydrodynamic description through a coarse-graining procedure and perturbative approach.
model_or_system: A 2D system of N anisotropic, pulsating, active particles (ellipses) with internal degrees of freedom including position, orientation, and an internal phase.
key_observables: Phase synchronization (R_phi), nematic order (R_theta), current (Upsilon), and topological charge of defects (kappa).
important_parameters: Particle density (rho_0), synchronization strength (epsilon), deformation amplitude (lambda), and internal driving frequency (omega).
main_assumptions: The hydrodynamic model assumes constant density, neglects position-based interactions in simplified dynamics, and uses an analytical approximation for interparticle distance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates particle parametrization and deformation modes; Figure 2 shows phase diagrams for squeezing and stretching modes; Figure 4 presents hydrodynamic phase diagrams and numerical profiles of synchronization fields showing waves and defects.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem and particle model, presents numerical simulations and phase diagrams, derives a hydrodynamic description via coarse-graining, performs linear stability analysis, and concludes with a discussion of results and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Parametrization of anisotropic deformable particles. (a) Each ellipse has four degrees of freedom: (i) the position r = (x, y) of their center that evolves in two dimensions, the orientation θ of the long axis l, and the internal degree of freedom ϕ that controls either the long or short axis, respectively l(ϕ) and s(ϕ) [Eqs. (4) and (5)]. (b-d) Elliptical deformation as a function of the internal phase ϕ ∈[0, 2π). For squeezing (resp. stretching) deformation, particles are round (resp. most elliptical) in their maximum size (green, ϕ = π/2), and most elliptical (resp. round) in their minimum size (dark blue, ϕ = 3π/2).

Figure 2. Phase diagrams in terms of synchronisation Rϕ [Eq. (7)], nematic order Rθ [Eq. (8)], and current Υ [Eq. (9)] for squeezing and stretching deformation models (resp. top and bottom rows). Both models feature arrest, cycle, and wave states in comparable parameter regimes. Only the squeezing deformation model entails a nematic phase (Rθ ≃1) at a very high density. Parameters: ω = 10, λ = 0.1, L = 100, l0 = 0.5. Panels (c,f): ε = 10.

Figure 3. (a,b) Patterns observed in the squeezing deformation model for (ρ, ε) = (1.8, 16), see black diamond in Fig. 2(a). (c-h) Increasing the deformation constant λ [Eqs. (4-5)] makes wavefronts thinner for squeezing and stretching deformation models (resp. top and bottom rows), and yields topological defects marked with black triangles: upwards for clockwise-rotating defects, and downwards for counterclockwise-rotating defects. At λ = 0.1, defects form in the squeezing deformation model, but none appear in the stretching deformation model. Parameters: ρ = 1.74, ε = 7.5, ω = 10, l0 = 0.5.

Figure 4. Hydrodynamic phase diagrams for the cases of (a) squeezing and (b) stretching deformation models. Black lines delinate the regions of existence of disordered (PDNO refers to phase disorder with nematic order), arrested, and cycling (clockwise and counter-clockwise) states at ν = 0; see dashed [Eq. (28)], dotted and dash-dotted [Eq. (29)], and solid [Eq. (30)] lines. To first order in ν, the linear stability analysis of the fixed-points reveals that nematic order emerges at a smaller density for squeezing than for stretching deformation.
